flashcards.definition: Earth-centered model of the universe championed by Ptolemy; dominated for ~1,400 years.
Sun-centered model proposed by Copernicus; confirmed by Galileo's observations.
Three laws describing planetary motion: elliptical orbits, equal areas, period-distance relationship.
Every mass attracts every other mass; F = G·m₁·m₂/r²; explains orbital mechanics.
First to use a telescope for astronomy; observed Jupiter's moons, Venus phases, and lunar craters.
Uses lenses to gather and focus light; suffers from chromatic aberration for large sizes.
Uses mirrors to gather light; avoids chromatic aberration; can be built much larger than refractors.
Detects radio-wavelength emissions from space; works through clouds and daylight.
Optical/UV/IR space telescope launched 1990; above atmospheric distortion for clear images.
James Webb Space Telescope; infrared optimized, 6.5 m mirror; studies early universe, exoplanets.
Average distance from Earth to the Sun; ~150 million km (93 million miles).
Distance light travels in one year; ~9.46 × 10¹² km; used for stellar and galactic distances.
Distance at which 1 AU subtends 1 arcsecond of parallax; ~3.26 light-years.
The sphere of space from which light has had time to reach us; ~93 billion ly in diameter.
Apparent shift in position of a nearby star against distant background stars as Earth orbits the Sun.
Systematic approach: observation → hypothesis → prediction → testing → conclusion → peer review.
A testable, falsifiable explanation for an observed phenomenon.
Gathering data from natural phenomena without experimental manipulation; astronomy relies heavily on this.
Process where other scientists evaluate research for accuracy and validity before publication.
A well-tested, broadly accepted explanation supported by extensive evidence (e.g., Big Bang theory).
Measurement of the brightness (flux) of celestial objects; uses apparent and absolute magnitudes.
Analysis of light spectra to determine composition, temperature, density, and velocity.
Precise measurement of star positions and proper motions; key for parallax and exoplanet detection.
Change in observed wavelength due to relative motion: blueshift (approaching) or redshift (receding).
How bright a star appears from Earth; depends on luminosity and distance.
Neolithic monument aligned with solstice positions; functioned as a prehistoric astronomical calendar.
First visible pre-dawn rising of Sirius; used by Egyptians to predict Nile flooding seasons.
Mesoamerican civilization with precise calendars, eclipse predictions, and Venus tracking.
Points where the Sun reaches its highest/lowest noon altitude; longest/shortest days.
Study of how ancient cultures understood and used celestial phenomena.
Mass of the Sun; ≈ 1.989 × 10³⁰ kg; used as a standard unit for stellar masses.
Luminosity of the Sun; ≈ 3.828 × 10²⁶ Watts; standard for comparing star brightnesses.
m − M = 5 log₁₀(d/10); relates apparent magnitude, absolute magnitude, and distance in parsecs.
λ_max = b/T; hotter objects peak at shorter (bluer) wavelengths; b ≈ 2.898 × 10⁶ nm·K.
L = 4πR²σT⁴; luminosity depends on radius squared and surface temperature to the fourth power.
Time for one full 360° rotation relative to stars; ~23 h 56 min.
Time from noon to noon (~24 h); slightly longer than sidereal due to Earth's orbital advance.
Closest point of Earth's orbit to the Sun (~Jan 3, ~147 million km).
Farthest point of Earth's orbit from the Sun (~Jul 4, ~152 million km).
Deflection of moving objects due to Earth's rotation; right in NH, left in SH.
~June 21 in NH; longest day; Sun highest in sky; most direct sunlight.
~Dec 21 in NH; shortest day; Sun lowest in sky; least direct sunlight.
~March 20 and Sept 22; Sun directly over equator; ~equal day and night globally.
Angle at which sunlight strikes Earth's surface; more direct = more energy per unit area.
Latitudes 66.5° N/S; mark boundaries of 24-h daylight/darkness at solstices.
Time between identical phases of the Moon as seen from Earth; ~29.5 days.
Moon between Sun and Earth; illuminated side faces away; Moon not visible.
Earth between Sun and Moon; fully illuminated face visible from Earth.
Increasing illumination (new → full); visible portion growing night by night.
Moon's rotation period equals its orbital period; same hemisphere always faces Earth.
Moon's shadow falls on Earth; Sun is blocked. Total (umbra) or partial (penumbra).
Earth's shadow falls on the Moon; Moon darkens or turns red.
Darkest central part of a shadow; total eclipse visible within the umbra.
Lighter outer part of a shadow; partial eclipse visible within the penumbra.
Point where the Moon's orbit crosses the ecliptic plane; eclipses occur near nodes.
Differential gravitational force across an object's diameter; stretches the object.
Extra-large tidal range when Sun and Moon are aligned (new or full Moon).
Smaller tidal range when Sun and Moon are at 90° (first/third quarter Moon).
Elongation of Earth's water toward (and away from) the Moon due to differential gravity.
Gravitational effect that synchronized the Moon's rotation with its orbit around Earth.
Narrow ground track (~100 km) where a total solar eclipse is visible.
Full Moon near perigee; appears up to ~14% larger and ~30% brighter than average.
Closest point of the Moon's orbit to Earth (~357,000 km).
Farthest point of the Moon's orbit from Earth (~406,000 km).
Exceptionally high tide during perigean spring tides; exacerbated by sea level rise.
T² ∝ a³; relates orbital period to semi-major axis; with Newton: T² = (4π²/GM)a³.
6.674 × 10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg²; universal constant in Newton's law of gravity.
Speed needed to maintain a circular orbit: v = √(GM/r); decreases with distance.
Minimum speed to escape a body's gravity: v_esc = √(2GM/r); independent of projectile mass.
U = −Gm₁m₂/r; negative because work must be done to separate masses.
Solar system formed from a collapsing, rotating cloud of gas and dust ~4.6 Gya.
Flattened disk of gas and dust orbiting the young Sun; planets formed within it.
Process of growth by collisions: dust → planetesimals → protoplanets → planets.
Distance from the Sun beyond which water and volatiles could condense as ice (~3-5 AU).
Period ~4.1–3.8 Gya of intense asteroid/comet impacts throughout the inner solar system.
Rocky planet with solid surface; relatively small with high density (Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars).
Positive feedback loop on Venus: CO₂ traps heat → oceans evaporate → more greenhouse gases → hotter.
Largest known volcano in the solar system, on Mars; ~22 km high, ~600 km wide.
Earth's lithosphere is divided into moving plates; drives earthquakes, volcanism, mountain building.
Rotation opposite to orbital direction; Venus rotates clockwise (east to west); very slow (~243 Earth days).
Large, gaseous planet with low density, thick atmosphere, and many moons (Jupiter, Saturn).
Outer planets (Uranus, Neptune) with interiors rich in water, ammonia, and methane ices.
Jupiter's massive anticyclonic storm; larger than Earth; observed for over 350 years.
Jupiter's four largest moons discovered by Galileo: Io, Europa, Ganymede, Callisto.
Disk of ice and rock particles orbiting a planet; Saturn's are the most prominent.
Orbits the Sun, spherical, but hasn't cleared its orbital neighborhood (e.g., Pluto, Ceres).
Region between Mars and Jupiter containing most asteroids; total mass < Earth's Moon.
Asteroid with orbit that brings it close to Earth's orbit; monitoring for impact risk.
Carbonaceous asteroid; most common type; dark, primitive composition.
NASA's Double Asteroid Redirection Test (2022); demonstrated kinetic impactor deflection of asteroid Dimorphos.
Icy body from the Kuiper Belt or Oort Cloud; develops coma and tails when near the Sun.
Spherical shell of icy bodies at ~2,000–200,000 AU; source of long-period comets.
Disk of icy objects beyond Neptune (~30–50 AU); source of short-period comets and dwarf planets.
Streak of light (shooting star) produced when a meteoroid burns up in Earth's atmosphere.
Increased meteor activity when Earth passes through a comet's debris trail.
Minimum speed for gas molecules to escape a planet's gravity; determines atmospheric retention.
Atmospheric gases (CO₂, H₂O, CH₄) trap infrared radiation → surface warming.
Technique for analyzing exoplanet atmospheres by observing starlight filtered through them during transit.
Height over which atmospheric pressure drops by a factor of e (~2.72); indicates atmosphere thickness.
Mix of gases in a planet's atmosphere; determined by mass, temperature, volcanism, and biology.
Gold-plated phonograph records aboard Voyager 1 & 2 carrying sounds and images of Earth for potential alien finders.
Joint NASA/ESA mission; orbited Saturn 2004–2017; Huygens landed on Titan.
NASA Mars rover (2021–present); collecting samples, studying geology, and searching for biosignatures.
First aircraft to achieve powered flight on another planet (Mars, 2021); originally a tech demo.
NASA probe that flew by Pluto (2015) and Kuiper Belt object Arrokoth (2019).
Planets orbit in ellipses with the Sun at one focus.
A planet sweeps equal areas in equal time; moves faster at perihelion, slower at aphelion.
T² = a³ (simplified); period squared is proportional to semi-major axis cubed.
Half the longest diameter of an ellipse; average distance from the Sun; denoted 'a'.
Measure of how elongated an ellipse is; 0 = circle, close to 1 = very elongated.
Sun's center; ~15 million K; site of hydrogen fusion; produces 99% of the Sun's energy.
Layer where energy travels outward via photon absorption and re-emission.
Outer zone where hot plasma rises and cool plasma sinks; creates granulation.
Visible surface of the Sun; ~5,800 K; shows sunspots and granulation.
Outermost solar atmosphere; ~1–3 million K; visible during total solar eclipses.
Primary fusion reaction in the Sun; four hydrogen nuclei fuse to form one helium nucleus + energy.
Einstein's equation; mass deficit is converted to energy in nuclear reactions.
Difference between the mass of reactants and products in a nuclear reaction; released as energy.
Nearly massless, chargeless particle produced in fusion; escapes the Sun almost immediately.
Total energy output of a star per unit time; Sun's luminosity = 3.8 × 10²⁶ W.
Cooler, darker region on the photosphere caused by strong magnetic fields (~3,500–4,500 K).
~11-year cyclical variation in sunspot number and solar activity; magnetic polarity reverses every cycle.
Sudden, intense burst of electromagnetic radiation from the Sun's surface; powered by magnetic reconnection.
Massive expulsion of plasma and magnetic field from the corona; can cause geomagnetic storms.
Arc of dense, relatively cool plasma suspended above the photosphere by magnetic fields.
Stream of charged particles (plasma) flowing from the Sun's corona at 400–800 km/s.
Bubble of space dominated by the solar wind; extends to ~120 AU; Voyager 1 & 2 have crossed its boundary.
The spiral shape of the interplanetary magnetic field due to the Sun's rotation and outward-flowing solar wind.
The mechanism generating the Sun's magnetic field through differential rotation and convection of conducting plasma.
Boundary where the solar wind pressure equals the interstellar medium pressure; edge of the heliosphere.
Total solar energy per unit area at Earth's distance; ~1,361 W/m²; varies ~0.1% over the solar cycle.
Period (1645–1715) of very few sunspots; possibly linked to the Little Ice Age (correlation debated).
Disturbance of Earth's magnetosphere caused by solar wind/CME; can affect power grids and communications.
Major geomagnetic storm caused a 12-hour power outage affecting 6 million people in Québec, Canada.
Conditions in space (solar wind, CMEs, radiation) that affect Earth's technology and human spaceflight.
Solar Dynamics Observatory (NASA, 2010); continuous high-resolution solar imaging in multiple wavelengths.
NASA probe making the closest passes to the Sun; studying corona and solar wind in situ.
Solar and Heliospheric Observatory (ESA/NASA, 1995); studies the Sun from the L1 Lagrange point.
Early 1600s telescope observations demonstrating the Sun has blemishes and rotates.
Analyzing sunlight's spectrum to determine the Sun's composition, temperature, and magnetic fields.
L = 4πR²σT⁴; luminosity depends on surface area and temperature to the 4th power.
F = L/(4πd²); observed brightness decreases with the square of the distance.
λ_max = 2.898 × 10⁶ / T (nm, K); hotter objects peak at shorter wavelengths.
Idealized emission spectrum depending only on temperature; stars approximate blackbodies.
E = mc²; mass can be converted to energy; explains stellar energy production.
Classification of stars by temperature/absorption lines: O B A F G K M (hot to cool).
Total energy radiated per second; measured in solar luminosities (L☉) or watts.
Brightness of a star if placed at 10 parsecs; measures intrinsic luminosity.
Brightness as seen from Earth; depends on luminosity AND distance.
Apparent shift of a nearby star against distant background; used to measure distance (d = 1/p parsecs).
Plot of luminosity vs temperature; reveals evolutionary stages and stellar classes.
Diagonal band on the HR diagram where stars fuse hydrogen; ~90% of all stars.
Upper-right of HR diagram; cool but very luminous stars (large radius).
Lower-left of HR diagram; hot but dim stars (very small radius).
Vertical region on the HR diagram where pulsating variable stars (e.g., Cepheids) are found.
Cold, dense region of gas (mostly H₂) and dust where stars form; also called stellar nurseries.
Collapsing core of gas and dust that hasn't yet started hydrogen fusion; heated by gravitational contraction.
Young, pre-main-sequence star exhibiting variability, strong winds, and a surrounding protoplanetary disk.
Minimum mass of a gas cloud needed for gravitational collapse to overcome thermal pressure.
Small, bright nebulosity produced by jets from protostars hitting surrounding gas.
Evolutionary phase where stars fuse hydrogen; longest phase of a star's life (~90%).
L ∝ M^3.5 for main sequence stars; higher mass = dramatically higher luminosity.
Time on the main sequence; t ∝ M/L ∝ M^−2.5; massive stars live shorter lives.
Low-mass (< 0.5 M☉), cool M-type main sequence star; extremely long-lived and most common star type.
Balance between gravity (inward) and radiation/gas pressure (outward) in a stable star.
Post-main-sequence star with expanded, cooled outer layers and a contracting, hot core.
Explosive onset of He fusion in the degenerate core of a low-mass red giant.
Fusion of three helium-4 nuclei into carbon-12; occurs in red giant cores.
Shell of gas expelled by a dying low-mass star; reveals the hot white dwarf core.
Maximum mass of a white dwarf (~1.4 M☉); above this, electron degeneracy cannot support it.
Explosion of a massive star (>8 M☉) when its iron core collapses; Type II supernova.
Ultra-dense remnant of a supernova; ~20 km; supported by neutron degeneracy pressure.
Rotating neutron star emitting beams of radiation; observed as periodic pulses.
Rapid neutron capture in supernovae/mergers; creates heavy elements like gold, platinum, uranium.
Expanding shell of gas and dust from a supernova explosion (e.g., Crab Nebula).
Boundary around a black hole beyond which escape is impossible; the 'point of no return.'
Radius of the event horizon: r_s = 2GM/c²; defines the size of a non-rotating black hole.
Point of theoretically infinite density at the center of a black hole (classical GR prediction).
Disk of infalling matter spiraling around a black hole; heats up and emits X-rays.
Supermassive black hole at the Milky Way's center; ~4 million M☉; imaged by the EHT.
Red supergiant in Orion, ~700 light-years away; candidate for a future supernova.
Ultra-cool red dwarf with 7 Earth-sized planets; key target for habitability studies.
First neutron star merger detected by LIGO/Virgo AND observed electromagnetically; confirmed r-process nucleosynthesis.
Observing cosmic events using multiple signals: light, gravitational waves, neutrinos, cosmic rays.
Detection of exoplanets by measuring the dimming of a star as a planet passes in front of it.
Disk-shaped galaxy with spiral arms, central bulge, and active star formation (e.g., Milky Way, Andromeda).
Smooth, featureless galaxy of mostly old red stars; little gas or star formation; E0–E7.
Galaxy with no definite shape; often gas-rich with active star formation (e.g., Magellanic Clouds).
Spiral galaxy with a bar-shaped structure through its center; Milky Way is a barred spiral.
Classification diagram for galaxies: ellipticals on left, spirals branching right (normal and barred).
Thin, flat region of the Milky Way containing spiral arms, young stars, gas, and dust.
Central region of the Milky Way; older stars, bar structure, and the supermassive black hole Sgr A*.
Spherical region surrounding the disk; contains old stars, globular clusters, and dark matter.
The spiral arm of the Milky Way where the Sun is located; a minor arm (spur).
Dense, spherical cluster of ~100,000–1 million old stars orbiting in the galactic halo.
Gravitationally bound collection of hundreds to thousands of galaxies; contain hot intracluster gas and dark matter.
The galaxy group containing the Milky Way, Andromeda, Triangulum, and ~80 smaller galaxies.
Large-scale agglomeration of galaxy clusters and groups (e.g., Laniakea Supercluster).
The large-scale structure of the universe: filaments, walls, and voids forming a web-like pattern.
Vast, nearly empty region of space between filaments in the cosmic web; can be hundreds of Mly across.
Invisible mass (~27% of universe) that doesn't emit or absorb light; detected through gravitational effects.
Mysterious component (~68%) driving the accelerated expansion of the universe.
Plot of orbital velocity vs distance from galactic center; flat curves imply dark matter halos.
Bending of light from distant objects by massive foreground structures; reveals dark matter distribution.
Einstein's term representing a constant energy density filling space; a candidate for dark energy.
v = H₀d; recession velocity of a galaxy is proportional to its distance; describes the expanding universe.
Rate of expansion; currently ~70 km/s/Mpc; exact value debated ('Hubble tension').
The event ~13.8 billion years ago from which the universe expanded from a hot, dense state.
Relic radiation from ~380,000 years after the Big Bang; observed as ~2.7 K microwave radiation filling all of space.
Prediction that the early universe produced ~75% H and ~25% He (by mass); matches observations.
Long-exposure HST images of 'empty' sky revealing thousands of distant galaxies; showed galaxy evolution.
Deepest infrared image (2022); revealed extremely distant, unexpectedly massive early galaxies.
Gravitational-wave source whose distance can be inferred from the signal; independent H₀ measurement.
How galaxies form, grow, merge, and change over cosmic time.
The period when the first stars and galaxies formed, ~100–500 million years after the Big Bang.
v = H₀d; recession velocity = Hubble constant × distance; describes universal expansion.
z = Δλ/λ₀; shift of spectral lines to longer wavelengths due to recession velocity/expansion.
1 million parsecs (~3.26 million light-years); standard distance unit in extragalactic astronomy.
Speed at which a galaxy appears to move away due to the expansion of space.
Time since light left a distant object; higher redshift → longer lookback time → seeing the past.
First artificial satellite; launched by the USSR on October 4, 1957; started the Space Age.
First crewed Moon landing (July 20, 1969); Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin walked on the Moon.
NASA's reusable spacecraft (1981–2011); 135 missions; launched and serviced satellites, built the ISS.
International Space Station; collaborative orbiting lab (1998–present); 5 space agencies.
NASA program to return humans to the Moon and establish sustainable presence for future Mars missions.
Low Earth Orbit (~200–2,000 km); used by ISS, Hubble, Earth observation satellites.
Geostationary Earth Orbit (~35,786 km); satellite stays over one point; used for weather, communications.
Using a planet's gravity to accelerate or redirect a spacecraft without using fuel.
Unmanned robotic spacecraft sent to explore other celestial bodies.
Global Positioning System; ~31 satellites in MEO providing precise location data worldwide.
Near-weightlessness experienced in orbit; causes physiological changes (bone loss, muscle atrophy).
Galactic cosmic rays and solar particle events; major health risk for astronauts beyond LEO.
Environmental Control and Life Support System; provides air, water, temperature control on spacecraft.
~1–2% bone density loss per month in microgravity; similar to severe osteoporosis.
Signal travel time between Earth and distant spacecraft; Mars: 4–24 minutes one-way.
International Space Station; orbiting laboratory at ~400 km; 5 agency cooperation; occupied since 2000.
Experiments conducted in near-weightlessness; unique conditions impossible to replicate on Earth.
Standard crew rotation on the ISS; typically 6 months; numbered sequentially.
Robotic arm on the ISS (CSA); used for station maintenance, moving modules, and capturing spacecraft.
NASA program using private spacecraft (SpaceX Dragon, Boeing Starliner) to ferry astronauts to the ISS.
Planned small space station in lunar orbit; staging point for Moon surface missions and Mars preparation.
SpaceX's fully reusable super heavy-lift launch vehicle; designed for Moon and Mars missions.
In-Situ Resource Utilization; using local resources (lunar ice, Martian CO₂) to produce fuel, water, oxygen.
NASA mission to study Jupiter's moon Europa's subsurface ocean and habitability (launch ~2024).
Proposed interstellar mission using light sails accelerated by lasers to reach Alpha Centauri.
SpaceX reusable rocket; first-stage booster lands and is reflown; >200 landings by 2024.
James Webb Space Telescope; 6.5 m infrared telescope at L2; studying the earliest galaxies, exoplanet atmospheres, and star formation.
Mars helicopter; first powered flight on another planet; completed 72 flights (2021–2024).
Ability to reflown rocket components; dramatically reduces launch costs (SpaceX pioneered orbital-class reuse).
JWST's tennis-court-sized, 5-layer sunshield that keeps instruments at ~-233°C.
Δv = v_e × ln(m₀/m_f); relates change in velocity to exhaust velocity and mass ratio.
Measure of rocket engine efficiency; I_sp = v_e/g₀ (seconds); higher = more efficient.
Force produced by a rocket engine: F = ṁ × v_e (mass flow rate × exhaust velocity).
Total change in velocity a spacecraft can achieve; determines what missions are possible.
m₀/m_f = initial mass / final mass (after fuel burn); higher ratio → more Δv.
Region around a star where surface temperatures allow liquid water; also called the Goldilocks zone.
Considered essential for life as we know it; excellent solvent for biochemical reactions.
Process where organisms derive energy from chemical reactions (e.g., oxidizing hydrogen sulfide) rather than sunlight.
Carbon-based compounds (amino acids, sugars, lipids, nucleotides) — building blocks of life.
Hypothesis that life or its precursors could be distributed throughout the universe via meteorites or comets.
Organism that thrives in physically or geochemically extreme conditions lethal to most life.
Organism thriving at temperatures >60°C; found in hot springs and hydrothermal vents.
Bacterium that survives extreme radiation (~5,000× human lethal dose); repairs its own DNA.
Microscopic animal surviving extreme cold, heat, vacuum, radiation, and desiccation (cryptobiosis).
Organism living inside rocks or in pore spaces within minerals; found kilometers underground.
NASA Mars rover (2021–present); collecting samples for future return; MOXIE and Ingenuity included.
NASA orbiter mission to study Europa's ice shell and subsurface ocean; launch 2024.
Analyzing starlight filtered through a planet's atmosphere during transit to identify chemical composition.
Mars meteorite found in Antarctica; controversial claim of fossil microbes (1996); debated.
Fell in Australia (1969); contains amino acids and organic compounds — supports panspermia hypothesis.
Search for ExtraTerrestrial Intelligence; systematic effort to detect artificial signals from alien civilizations.
Radio frequency band (1420–1720 MHz) considered optimal for interstellar communication (low noise).
Contradiction between the high probability of alien civilizations and the lack of evidence; 'Where is everybody?'
N = R* × f_p × n_e × f_l × f_i × f_c × L; estimates the number of communicating civilizations in the Milky Way.
A strong, narrowband radio signal detected in 1977 at Ohio State; never repeated; origin still unknown.
Planet orbiting a star other than our Sun; over 5,500 confirmed (as of 2024).
Detecting exoplanets by measuring the slight dimming of a star as a planet crosses in front.
Detecting exoplanets by measuring the Doppler shift in a star's spectrum caused by gravitational wobble.
Ultracool dwarf star with 7 Earth-sized planets; 3 in the habitable zone; prime JWST target.
NASA mission (2009–2018) that discovered thousands of exoplanets using the transit method.
Mars landing site for Perseverance; ancient lake/river delta — ideal for preserving biosignatures.
Planned mission to bring Perseverance's cached samples back to Earth for advanced laboratory analysis.
Geysers erupting from Enceladus's south pole containing water, organic molecules, and H₂ — accessible for sampling.
NASA rotorcraft mission to Titan; will explore organic-rich surface and search for prebiotic chemistry.
Features suggesting an environment could support life: liquid water, energy, organic molecules, suitable chemistry.

u8_l8.7.title: AP Prep: Probability Models for Life

flashcards.definition: N = R* × f_p × n_e × f_l × f_i × f_c × L; framework for estimating communicating civilizations.
Average rate of star formation in the Milky Way: ~1.5–3 solar masses per year.
Average number of habitable planets per star with planets; estimated 0.2–0.5 based on Kepler data.
Average number of years a civilization remains detectable; most uncertain Drake variable.
Statistical method for updating probability estimates as new data is obtained; used in modern astrobiology.
Uses mirrors to focus light; all major modern telescopes are reflectors (no chromatic aberration, scalable).
Technology using deformable mirrors to correct atmospheric distortion in real time; achieves near-space quality.
Diameter of a telescope's primary mirror or lens; larger aperture = more light and resolution.
Combining signals from multiple telescopes to achieve the resolution of a much larger telescope.
Extremely Large Telescope (ESO); 39 m primary mirror; under construction in Chile's Atacama Desert.
NASA/ESA; 2.4 m; UV/vis/near-IR; launched 1990; revolutionized astronomy; serviced 5× by shuttle.
James Webb Space Telescope; 6.5 m infrared; at L2; launched 2021; studying earliest galaxies and exoplanets.
Sun-Earth gravitational balance point ~1.5 million km from Earth; JWST's orbit location.
Image of a tiny sky patch revealing thousands of galaxies — showed the universe is filled with galaxies.
JWST's beryllium mirrors coated with thin gold layer — optimized for reflecting infrared light.
Telescope detecting radio-wavelength electromagnetic radiation; can observe through dust and clouds.
Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array; 66 dishes in Chile; studies star/planet formation in dusty regions.
Square Kilometre Array; massive international radio telescope under construction; unprecedented sensitivity.
Rapidly rotating neutron star emitting beams of radio waves; discovered by Jocelyn Bell Burnell in 1967.
Brief, intense burst of radio emission from distant sources; origin still being studied (magnetars suspected).
Analysis of electromagnetic radiation by wavelength; reveals composition, temperature, density, motion.
Dark lines in a spectrum where specific wavelengths are absorbed by cooler gas; identify elements.
Bright lines in a spectrum at specific wavelengths emitted by hot gas; identify elements and temperature.
Change in wavelength due to relative motion; blueshift = approaching, redshift = receding.
Continuous spectrum emitted by a hot, dense object; peak wavelength depends on temperature (Wien's law).
Computational model tracking gravitational interactions of millions to billions of particles.
Modeling fluid dynamics (gas and plasma) in astrophysical objects like stars and accretion disks.
AI algorithms classifying galaxies, detecting exoplanet transits, identifying transient events in massive datasets.
Upcoming survey telescope (LSST); will image the entire visible sky every few nights; massive data output.
High-performance computing system essential for running large-scale astrophysical simulations.
Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory; first detected gravitational waves on Sept 14, 2015.
First gravitational wave detection from a neutron star merger (2017); observed in light too — multi-messenger astronomy.
Earth-sized virtual radio telescope using VLBI; imaged M87* (2019) and Sgr A* (2022) black holes.
Combining gravitational waves, electromagnetic radiation, and neutrinos to study a single event.
Kepler/TESS/JWST have transformed exoplanet science from detection to atmospheric characterization.

u9_l9.7.title: AP Prep: Data Analysis in Astronomy

flashcards.definition: Graph of brightness vs. time; used to detect transits, eclipses, and stellar variability.
Fractional decrease in star brightness during planet transit; depth = (R_planet/R_star)².
Δλ/λ = v/c; Doppler shift reveals how fast an object moves toward or away from the observer.
Assessment of measurement uncertainties; includes random error (precision) and systematic error (accuracy).
v = H₀ × d; recession velocity is proportional to distance; H₀ ≈ 70 km/s/Mpc.
Three laws of planetary motion: elliptical orbits, equal areas in equal times, P² ∝ a³.
Hertzsprung-Russell diagram plotting luminosity vs. temperature; reveals stellar classification and evolution.
v = H₀d; galaxy recession velocity proportional to distance; evidence for expanding universe.
N = R* × f_p × n_e × f_l × f_i × f_c × L; framework for estimating communicating civilizations.
Analyzing light by wavelength to determine composition, temperature, velocity, and other properties.
Read carefully, eliminate wrong answers, check units, manage time, answer every question.
Removing clearly incorrect answers increases probability of selecting the correct one.
Checking that units in calculations are consistent; powerful error-detection tool.
Rough estimation to check if an answer is reasonable (powers of 10).
Allocate roughly equal time per question; flag difficult ones and return later.
Applying multiple concepts to a real-world scenario; requires identifying relevant data and principles.
Breaking complex problems into sequential steps, each applying a specific principle.
Extracting meaning from graphs, spectra, light curves, or numerical data.
Supporting conclusions with evidence and physical reasoning.
Connecting concepts from different units to analyze a single scenario.
GPS satellites must account for time dilation (special + general relativity) — clocks run differently in orbit.
Double Asteroid Redirection Test; NASA kinetic impactor that successfully changed asteroid Dimorphos's orbit in 2022.
Charge-Coupled Devices; developed for astronomy, now standard in digital cameras and smartphones.
Solar activity (flares, CMEs) affecting Earth's technology — power grids, satellites, communications.
Understanding planetary atmospheres and solar variability helps model and predict Earth's climate.

u10_l10.5.title: Capstone: Connecting Astronomy to Other Sciences

flashcards.definition: Creation of elements inside stars; lighter elements in main sequence, heavier in supernovae and mergers.
Astronomy connects physics, chemistry, biology, geology, engineering, and computer science.
Some caused by asteroid impacts (e.g., Chicxulub ~66 Mya) — astronomy-Earth connection.
Powers Earth's interior (plate tectonics, magnetic field); elements created in stellar nucleosynthesis.
Understanding our place in the universe — from subatomic particles to the cosmic web.
L = 4πR²σT⁴; luminosity depends on radius squared and temperature to the fourth power.
Brightness decreases with the square of distance: b = L/(4πd²).
d(pc) = 1/p(arcsec); fundamental distance measurement for nearby stars.
λ_max = b/T; peak wavelength inversely proportional to temperature (b = 2.9 × 10⁶ nm·K).
Big Bang → atoms → stars → heavy elements → planets → molecules → life → intelligence.
